content.assessment: A measurement and monitoring architecture does not itself alter the physical school network, transport arrangements, or specialist deployment, but it is a necessary precondition for identifying where geographic access and capacity constraints are most severe. The lens's own limitation applies directly here: better data cannot establish delivery capability, only illuminate it. Reconciling divergent series and decomposing rule-change effects (e.g., the contested 2020 readiness-rule tightening) could reveal which regions' rising identification reflects genuine unmet need versus definitional artifact, informing subsequent network-capacity decisions.
opportunities: Improved data could support targeted network investment decisions in underserved regions once workforce and infrastructure limitations are known.
strengths: Could integrate county-level prevalence variance data (15.6% vs 6.2%) with network-capacity data to identify where access gaps are most acute.

Decomposition of the contested 2020 rule-change effect could clarify whether observed identification changes reflect true demand shifts relevant to network planning or artifacts of gatekeeping policy.
threats: Without paired capacity investment, better data alone risks becoming an administrative exercise disconnected from actual geographic service delivery.
weaknesses: A monitoring architecture has no direct mechanism to expand transport entitlements, training-program coverage, or specialist capacity; it is diagnostic, not operational.
The finding on unmeasured need among multiply disadvantaged families' children indicates that even reconciled official statistics will systematically undercount true network demand.
